expected_ranked_descriptions: Metformin mechanism of action: Metformin's mechanisms of action are unique from other classes of oral antihyperglycemic drugs. Metformin decreases blood glucose levels by decreasing hepatic glucose production (gluconeogenesis), decreasing the intestinal absorption of glucose, and increasing insulin sensitivity by increasing peripheral glucose uptake and utilization. It is well established that metformin inhibits mitochondrial complex I activity, and it has since been generally postulated that its potent antidiabetic effects occur through this mechanism. The above processes lead to a decrease in blood glucose, managing type II diabetes and exerting positive effects on glycemic control.

Metformin is indicated for type 2 diabetes mellitus and diabetes mellitus (disease), targeting GPD1, PRKAB1, and ETFDH. Effects include Abdominal distention, Abdominal pain, Angina pectoris, and Poor appetite. Contraindicated in liver failure, kidney disease, and pyoureter.
Metformin is approved for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus and diabetes mellitus (disease)

Metformin pharmacological category: Metformin is part of Alimentary Tract and Metabolism ; Amidines ; Biguanides ; Blood Glucose Lowering Agents ; Drugs that are Mainly Renally Excreted ; Drugs Used in Diabetes ; Guanidines ; MATE 1 Substrates ; MATE 2 Substrates ; MATE substrates ; OCT1 substrates ; OCT2 Inhibitors ; OCT2 Substrates.

expected_ranked_descriptions: Symptoms of myocardial infarction (disease): Common heart attack signs and symptoms include: Pressure, tightness, pain, or a squeezing or aching sensation in your chest or arms that may spread to your neck, jaw or back, Nausea, indigestion, heartburn or abdominal pain, Shortness of breath, Cold sweat, Fatigue, Lightheadedness or sudden dizziness, Heart attack symptoms vary, Not all people who have heart attacks have the same symptoms or have the same severity of symptoms. Some people have mild pain; others have more severe pain. Some people have no symptoms. For others, the first sign may be sudden cardiac arrest. However, the more signs and symptoms you have, the greater the chance you're having a heart attack. Some heart attacks strike suddenly, but many people have warning signs and symptoms hours, days or weeks in advance. The earliest warning might be recurrent chest pain or pressure that's triggered by activity and relieved by rest. Angina is caused by a temporary decrease in blood flow to the heart.

acute coronary syndrome: Signs and symptoms related to acute ischemia of the myocardium secondary to coronary artery disease. The clinical presentation covers a spectrum of heart diseases from unstable angina to myocardial infarction.
myocardial infarction (disease): A history of myocardial infarction in the absence of clinical symptoms and positive electrocardiographic findings.

Symptoms of acute myocarditis: If you have a mild case of myocarditis or are in the early stages, you might have no symptoms or mild ones, such as chest pain or shortness of breath. In serious cases, the signs and symptoms of myocarditis vary, depending on the cause of the disease. Common myocarditis signs and symptoms include: Chest pain, Rapid or abnormal heart rhythms, Shortness of breath, at rest or during physical activity, Fluid retention with swelling of your legs, ankles and feet, Fatigue, Other signs and symptoms of a viral infection, such as a headache, body aches, joint pain, fever, a sore throat or diarrhea, Myocarditis in children, When children develop myocarditis, they might have signs and symptoms including: Fever, Fainting, Breathing difficulties, Rapid breathing, Rapid or abnormal heart rhythms

Symptoms of myocardial ischemia: Some people who have myocardial ischemia don't have any signs or symptoms . When they do occur, the most common is chest pressure or pain, typically on the left side of the body . Other signs and symptoms — which might be experienced more commonly by women, older people and people with diabetes — include: Neck or jaw pain, Shoulder or arm pain, A fast heartbeat, Shortness of breath when you are physically active, Nausea and vomiting, Sweating, Fatigue

ent_hits: bacterial meningitis caused by gram-negative bacteria
expected_ranked_descriptions: bacterial meningitis commonly co-occurs with haemophilus meningitis, streptococcal meningitis, anaerobic meningitis, meningeal tuberculosis, and Escherichia coli meningitis
bacterial meningitis: Inflammation of the membranes surrounding the brain and spinal cord due to a bacterial infection.

tuberculous meningitis: A rare bacterial infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, characterized by a variable clinical picture comprising classic manifestations of meningitis, i. e. headache, fever, and stiff neck, in addition to cranial nerve palsies (most commonly III, VI, and VII), altered mental status, and seizures, among others. Basal meningeal enhancement in neuroimaging, cerebrospinal fluid abnormalities (moderate lymphocytic pleocytosis, moderately elevated protein concentration, low glucose), and a chest x-ray suggestive of pulmonary tuberculosis may support the diagnosis.

Causes of bacterial meningitis: Viral infections are the most common cause of meningitis, followed by bacterial infections and, rarely, fungal and parasitic infections. Because bacterial infections can be life-threatening, identifying the cause is essential. Bacterial meningitis, Bacteria that enter the bloodstream and travel to the brain and spinal cord cause acute bacterial meningitis. But it can also occur when bacteria directly invade the meninges. This may be caused by an ear or sinus infection, a skull fracture, or — rarely — some surgeries. Several strains of bacteria can cause acute bacterial meningitis, most commonly: Streptococcus pneumoniae . This bacterium is the most common cause of bacterial meningitis in infants, young children and adults in the United States. It more commonly causes pneumonia or ear or sinus infections. A vaccine can help prevent this infection. Neisseria meningitidis . This bacterium is another leading cause of bacterial meningitis. These bacteria commonly cause an upper respiratory infection but can cause meningococcal meningitis when they enter the bloodstream. This is a highly contagious infection that affects mainly teenagers and young adults.
bacterial meningitis caused by gram-negative bacteria commonly co-occurs with syphilitic meningitis, gram-negative bacterial infections, and bacterial meningitis

expected_ranked_descriptions: Warfarin mechanism of action: Warfarin is a antagonist which acts to inhibit the production of vitamin K by vitamin K epoxide reductase. The reduced form of vitamin K, vitamin KH 2 is a cofactor used in the γ-carboxylation of coagulation factors VII, IX, X, and thrombin. Carboxylation induces a conformational change allowing the factors to bind Ca 2+ and to phospholipid surfaces. Uncarboxylated factors VII, IX, X, and thrombin are biologically inactive and therefore serve to interrupt the coagulation cascade. The endogenous anticoagulation proteins C and S also require γ-carboxylation to function. This is particularly true in the case of thrombin which must be activated in order to form a thrombus.

Warfarin: Warfarin is an anticoagulant drug normally used to prevent blood clot formation as well as migration. Although originally marketed as a pesticide (d-Con, Rodex, among others), Warfarin has since become the most frequently prescribed oral anticoagulant in North America. Warfarin has several properties that should be noted when used medicinally, including its ability to cross the placental barrier during pregnancy which can result in fetal bleeding, spontaneous abortion, preterm birth, stillbirth, and neonatal death. Additional adverse effects such as necrosis, purple toe syndrome, osteoporosis, valve and artery calcification, and drug interactions have also been documented with warfarin use.
Warfarin targets ALB, ORM1, CYP1A2, and CYP3A4
Warfarin protein binding: 99% bound primarily to albumin.

Warfarin pharmacodynamics: Warfarin is an anticoagulant, as such it disrupts the coagulation cascade to reduce frequency and extent of thrombus formation. In patients with deep vein thrombosis or atrial fibrillation there is an increased risk of thrombus formation due to the reduced movement of blood. For patients with cardiac valve disease or valve replacements this increased coagulability is due to tissue damage. Thrombi due to venous thrombosis can travel to the lungs and become pulmonary emboli, blocking circulation to a portion of lung tissue. Thrombi which form in the heart can travel to the brain and cause ischemic strokes. Prevention of these events is the primary goal of warfarin therapy.

expected_ranked_descriptions: intrinsic asthma: An asthma that is triggered by factors not related to allergies such as anxiety, stress, exercise, cold air, dry air, hyperventilation, smoke, viruses, chemical irritants, autonomic imbalance, hormonal deficiencies and psychogenic influences. It is characterized by airway obstruction and inflammation that is at least partially reversible with medication. The symptoms include coughing, wheezing, shortness of breath or rapid breathing, and chest tightness.
Molecular basis of asthma involves ADCY2, ADCYAP1R1, PARP1, ADRB2, and ALDH2

hyperkalemic periodic paralysis, presenting with Episodic flaccid weakness, Myotonia, Hyperkalemia, and Infantile onset. Contraindicated: Guaifenesin and Guaiacol. Often co-occurs with familial periodic paralysis.

Management of hyperkalemic periodic paralysis: Management of patients consists in medical therapy and avoidance of triggering factors. Gentle physical activity, ingestion of carbohydrate-rich drinks/snacks or inhaled salbutamol at the onset of attacks may abort them. Daily use of carbonic anhydrase inhibitors or thiazide diuretics helps in preventing attacks. Dietary advice includes regular meals (to prevent fasting) and avoidance of potassium-rich foods.

expected_ranked_descriptions: Complications of diabetic ketoacidosis: Diabetic ketoacidosis is treated with fluids, electrolytes — such as sodium, potassium and chloride — and insulin. Perhaps surprisingly, the most common complications of diabetic ketoacidosis are related to this lifesaving treatment. Possible complications of the treatments, Treatment complications include: Low blood sugar . Insulin allows sugar to enter your cells, causing your blood sugar level to drop. If your blood sugar level drops too quickly, you can develop low blood sugar. Low potassium . The fluids and insulin used to treat diabetic ketoacidosis can cause your potassium level to drop too low. A low potassium level can impair the activities of your heart, muscles and nerves. To avoid this, electrolytes, including potassium are usually given along with fluid replacement as part of the treatment of diabetic ketoacidosis. Swelling in the brain . Adjusting your blood sugar level too quickly can produce swelling in your brain. This complication appears to be more common in children, especially those with newly diagnosed diabetes. Left untreated, the risks of diabetic ketoacidosis are much greater. Diabetic ketoacidosis can lead to loss of consciousness and, eventually, death.
diabetic ketoacidosis commonly co-occurs with type 2 diabetes mellitus and inborn errors of metabolism

Complications of diabetic nephropathy: Complications of diabetic nephropathy may develop gradually over months or years. They may include: Fluid retention, which could lead to swelling in your arms and legs, high blood pressure, or fluid in your lungs, A rise in potassium levels in your blood, Heart and blood vessel disease, possibly leading to stroke, Damage to the blood vessels of the retina, Anemia, Foot sores, erectile dysfunction, diarrhea and other problems related to damaged nerves and blood vessels, Pregnancy complications that carry risks for the mother and the developing fetus, Irreversible damage to your kidneys, eventually needing either dialysis or a kidney transplant for survival

Complications of microvascular complications of diabetes, susceptibility to: Long-term complications of diabetes develop gradually. The longer you have diabetes — and the less controlled your blood sugar — the higher the risk of complications. Eventually, diabetes complications may be disabling or even life-threatening. Possible complications include: Cardiovascular disease. Diabetes dramatically increases the risk of various cardiovascular problems, including coronary artery disease with chest pain, heart attack, stroke and narrowing of arteries . If you have diabetes, you're more likely to have heart disease or stroke., Nerve damage . Excess sugar can injure the walls of the tiny blood vessels that nourish your nerves, especially in your legs. This can cause tingling, numbness, burning or pain that usually begins at the tips of the toes or fingers and gradually spreads upward. Left untreated, you could lose all sense of feeling in the affected limbs. Damage to the nerves related to digestion can cause problems with nausea, vomiting, diarrhea or constipation. For men, it may lead to erectile dysfunction., Kidney damage . The kidneys contain millions of tiny blood vessel clusters that filter waste from your blood.

Complications of hyperglycemia: Long-term complications, Keeping your blood sugar in a healthy range can help prevent many diabetes-related complications. Long-term complications of untreated hyperglycemia can include: Cardiovascular disease, Nerve damage, Kidney damage or kidney failure, Damage to the blood vessels of the retina, potentially leading to blindness, Clouding of the normally clear lens of your eye, Feet problems caused by damaged nerves or poor blood flow that can lead to serious skin infections, ulcerations, and in some severe cases, amputation, Bone and joint problems, Teeth and gum infections, Emergency complications, If blood sugar rises high enough or for a prolonged period of time, it can lead to two serious conditions. Diabetic ketoacidosis. Diabetic ketoacidosis develops when you don't have enough insulin in your body. When this happens, sugar can't enter your cells for energy. Your blood sugar level rises, and your body begins to break down fat for energy. This process produces toxic acids known as ketones. Excess ketones accumulate in the blood and eventually "spill over" into the urine.

he_046933.anchor: familial hyperthyroidism due to mutations in TSH receptor
he_046933.description: familial hyperthyroidism due to mutations in TSH receptor, presenting with Delayed speech and language development, Motor delay, Hyperthyroidism, and Small for gestational age. Notably absent: Pretibial myxedema, Abnormal eye morphology, and Autoimmune antibody positivity. Often co-occurs with hypertension and hyperthyroidism.
entities: familial hyperthyroidism due to mutations in TSH receptor

he_046972.description: 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaric aciduria, presenting with Organic aciduria, Decreased plasma carnitine, Pallor, and Seizure. Notably absent: Ketonuria. Often co-occurs with disorder of fatty acid oxidation and ketogenesis and classic organic aciduria.
entities: disorder of fatty acid oxidation and ketogenesis

he_047046.description: glucocorticoid deficiency, presenting with Failure to thrive, Hyperkalemia, Seizure, and Hyponatremia. Notably absent: Abnormal circulating renin and Abnormal circulating aldosterone. Often co-occurs with familial glucocorticoid deficiency.

he_047050.description: ventricular tachycardia, familial, presenting with Paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia, Abnormality of metabolism/homeostasis, Autosomal dominant inheritance, and Sudden cardiac death. Notably absent: Cardiomyopathy. Often co-occurs with catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia and genetic cardiac rhythm disease.

he_047075.description: maturity-onset diabetes of the young, presenting with Infantile onset, Hyperglycemia, Type II diabetes mellitus, and Autosomal dominant inheritance. Notably absent: Abnormality of body mass index, Diabetic ketoacidosis, and Autoimmune antibody positivity. Often co-occurs with renal cysts and diabetes syndrome and Fanconi renotubular syndrome 4 with maturity-onset diabetes of the young.
entities: Fanconi renotubular syndrome 4 with maturity-onset diabetes of the young

he_047129.description: systemic primary carnitine deficiency disease, presenting with Lethargy, Encephalopathy, Hepatic steatosis, and Decreased plasma carnitine. Notably absent: Ketosis and Dicarboxylic aciduria. Often co-occurs with fatty acid oxidation and ketogenesis disorder with dilated cardiomyopathy and muscular lipidosis.
entities: fatty acid oxidation and ketogenesis disorder with dilated cardiomyopathy

he_047169.description: coronary artery disease, autosomal dominant, presenting with Hypercholesterolemia, Chest pain, Autosomal dominant inheritance, and Osteoporosis. Notably absent: Abnormality of cardiovascular system morphology, Abnormal heart morphology, and Type I diabetes mellitus. Often co-occurs with Mendelian disease and coronary artery disease.

he_047173.description: familial pseudohyperkalemia, presenting with Hand tremor, Generalized muscle weakness, Hyperkalemia, and Muscle spasm. Notably absent: Hemolytic anemia. Often co-occurs with hereditary stomatocytosis.

he_047223.description: cystoid macular edema, presenting with Strabismus, Pericentral retinitis pigmentosa, Hypermetropia, and Edema. Notably absent: Abnormal electroretinogram. Contraindicated: Glimepiride and Metformin. Often co-occurs with macular retinal edema and unclassified primitive or secondary maculopathy.

he_047248.description: pulmonary hypertension, primary, presenting with Pulmonary aterial intimal fibrosis, Elevated right atrial pressure, Dyspnea, and Pulmonary arterial hypertension. Notably absent: Thromboembolism. Often co-occurs with heritable pulmonary arterial hypertension.

he_047359.description: hereditary spastic paraplegia, presenting with Difficulty walking, Hyperactive patellar reflex, Inability to walk, and Polyneuropathy. Notably absent: Fasciculations, Seizure, and Abnormality of the cerebrospinal fluid. Often co-occurs with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease and pure hereditary spastic paraplegia.

he_047390.description: familial gestational hyperthyroidism, presenting with Increased circulating T4 level, Hyperthyroidism, Decreased thyroid-stimulating hormone level, and Tachycardia. Notably absent: Autoimmune antibody positivity. Often co-occurs with hypertension, pregnancy-induced and hypertension.

he_047413.description: pulmonary venoocclusive disease, presenting with Pulmonary venous occlusion, Centrilobular ground-glass opacification on pulmonary HRCT, Pulmonary arterial hypertension, and Elevated jugular venous pressure. Notably absent: Antinuclear antibody positivity and Cytoplasmic antineutrophil antibody positivity. Treatment includes Propranolol. Contraindicated: Ambrisentan and Bosentan. Often co-occurs with pulmonary veno-occlusive disease and/or pulmonary capillary haemangiomatosis and hypertension.
entities: Centrilobular ground-glass opacification on pulmonary HRCT
pulmonary veno-occlusive disease and/or pulmonary capillary haemangiomatosis

he_047414.description: late-onset isolated ACTH deficiency, presenting with Decreased circulating cortisol level, Adrenocorticotropin deficient adrenal insufficiency, Lethargy, and Muscle weakness. Notably absent: Hyperkalemia. Often co-occurs with acquired pituitary hormone deficiency.

he_047415.description: congenital isolated adrenocorticotropic hormone deficiency (disease), presenting with Cholestasis, Decreased circulating cortisol level, Seizure, and Infantile onset. Notably absent: Hyperkalemia. Often co-occurs with hypopituitarism and non-acquired pituitary hormone deficiency.

he_047432.description: incessant infant ventricular tachycardia, presenting with Ventricular tachycardia, Congestive heart failure, Cardiac arrest, and Prolonged QRS complex. Notably absent: Left ventricular systolic dysfunction. Contraindicated: Synephrine and Guaifenesin. Often co-occurs with non-genetic cardiac rhythm disease.

he_047450.description: spastic ataxia, presenting with Bilateral tonic-clonic seizure, Skeletal muscle atrophy, Dysphagia, and Progressive. Notably absent: Seizure, Abnormality of the cerebrospinal fluid, and Generalized amyotrophy. Often co-occurs with spasticity-ataxia-gait anomalies syndrome and autosomal dominant spastic ataxia.

he_047480.description: Tako-tsubo cardiomyopathy, presenting with Mildly reduced ejection fraction, T-wave inversion, ST segment elevation, and Chest pain. Notably absent: Coronary artery atherosclerosis, Coronary artery stenosis, and Abnormal coronary artery morphology. Often co-occurs with unclassified cardiomyopathy.

he_047495.anchor: deficiency in anterior pituitary function - variable immunodeficiency syndrome
he_047495.description: deficiency in anterior pituitary function - variable immunodeficiency syndrome, presenting with Recurrent otitis media, Diplopia, Global developmental delay, and Decreased response to growth hormone stimuation test. Notably absent: Vitiligo, Hyperkalemia, and Autoimmune antibody positivity. Often co-occurs with disease associated with non-acquired combined pituitary hormone deficiency and immunodeficiency, common variable.
entities: deficiency in anterior pituitary function - variable immunodeficiency syndrome
Decreased response to growth hormone stimuation test
disease associated with non-acquired combined pituitary hormone deficiency

he_047496.anchor: rapid-onset childhood obesity-hypothalamic dysfunction-hypoventilation-autonomic dysregulation syndrome
he_047496.description: rapid-onset childhood obesity-hypothalamic dysfunction-hypoventilation-autonomic dysregulation syndrome, presenting with Macrocephaly, Anteverted nares, Decreased lacrimation, and Emotional lability. Notably absent: Anxiety and Hyperkalemia. Often co-occurs with syndromic genetic obesity.
entities: rapid-onset childhood obesity-hypothalamic dysfunction-hypoventilation-autonomic dysregulation syndrome

he_047500.description: thyrotoxic periodic paralysis, presenting with Hyperthyroidism, Periodic hypokalemic paresis, Episodic hypokalemia, and Hyperhidrosis. Notably absent: Myotonia, Abnormality of peripheral nerve conduction, and Impaired myocardial contractility. Often co-occurs with thyrotoxic periodic paralysis, susceptibility to and familial periodic paralysis.

he_047589.description: long chain 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency, presenting with Decreased 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase level, Hypotonia, Hepatomegaly, and Pigmentary retinopathy. Often co-occurs with genetic peripheral neuropathy and fatty acid oxidation and ketogenesis disorder with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
entities: fatty acid oxidation and ketogenesis disorder with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy

he_047677.description: familial atrial fibrillation, presenting with Autosomal dominant inheritance, Permanent atrial fibrillation, Bradycardia, and Palpitations. Often co-occurs with atrial fibrillation (disease) and genetic cardiac rhythm disease.

he_047771.description: hyperkalemic periodic paralysis, presenting with Episodic flaccid weakness, Myotonia, Hyperkalemia, and Infantile onset. Contraindicated: Guaifenesin and Guaiacol. Often co-occurs with familial periodic paralysis.

he_047926.description: Brugada syndrome, presenting with ST segment elevation, Autosomal dominant inheritance, Bundle branch block, and Cardiac arrest. Often co-occurs with genetic cardiac rhythm disease and syndromic disease.

he_047936.description: type 2 diabetes mellitus, presenting with Late onset, Increased waist to hip ratio, Insulin resistance, and Type II diabetes mellitus. Treatment includes Troglitazone, Acetohexamide, and Chlorpropamide. Contraindicated: Dichlorophen and Diethylpropion. Risk factors: 2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic Acid, Agent Orange, and Air Pollutants. Often co-occurs with lipoatrophic diabetes and diabetes mellitus, noninsulin-dependent.

he_047997.description: monosodium glutamate sensitivity, presenting with Flushing, Dyspnea, Autosomal recessive inheritance, and Chest pain. Often co-occurs with Mendelian disease.

he_048060.description: arachnoiditis, presenting with Abnormality of the skeletal system, Abnormality of metabolism/homeostasis, Autosomal dominant inheritance, and Spastic paraparesis. Treatment includes Ceftizoxime and Kanamycin. Contraindicated: Carmustine. Often co-occurs with infectious meningitis.

he_048146.description: Liddle syndrome, presenting with Decreased circulating renin level, Metabolic alkalosis, Autosomal dominant inheritance, and Variable expressivity. Often co-occurs with hypertension and syndromic disease.

he_048176.description: combined malonic and methylmalonic acidemia, presenting with Ketoacidosis, Vomiting, Failure to thrive, and Global developmental delay. Often co-occurs with methylmalonic acidemia and classic organic aciduria.

he_048193.description: isovaleric acidemia, presenting with Lethargy, Ketoacidosis, Vomiting, and Leukopenia. Often co-occurs with classic organic aciduria.

he_048214.description: pseudohypoaldosteronism, presenting with Hyperkalemia, Pseudohypoaldosteronism, Hyperchloremic acidosis, and Periodic hyperkalemic paralysis. Often co-occurs with renal tubular transport disease and inherited renal tubular disease.

he_048232.description: glucocorticoid-remediable aldosteronism, presenting with Abnormality of the urinary system, Onset, Hyperaldosteronism, and Decreased circulating renin level. Often co-occurs with hypertension and familial hyperaldosteronism.

he_048417.description: inherited susceptibility to asthma, presenting with Multifactorial inheritance and Autosomal dominant inheritance. Often co-occurs with asthma-related traits, susceptibility to and Mendelian disease.

he_048458.description: acyl-CoA dehydrogenase 9 deficiency, presenting with Encephalopathy, Hepatic failure, Hypotonia, and Generalized hypotonia. Often co-occurs with fatty acid oxidation and ketogenesis disorder with dilated cardiomyopathy and mitochondrial disease with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
entities: fatty acid oxidation and ketogenesis disorder with dilated cardiomyopathy

he_048464.description: short QT syndrome, presenting with Shortened QT interval, Bradycardia, Syncope, and Sudden cardiac death. Often co-occurs with Mendelian disease and heart conduction disease.

he_048539.description: Jervell and Lange-Nielsen syndrome, presenting with Autosomal recessive inheritance, Prolonged QT interval, Syncope, and Sudden cardiac death. Often co-occurs with familial long QT syndrome and syndromic genetic deafness.

he_048578.description: Kasabach-Merritt syndrome, presenting with Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, Hyperkalemia, Ventricular arrhythmia, and Thrombocytopenia. Treatment includes Propranolol. Often co-occurs with Mendelian disease and hemorrhagic disorder due to a platelet anomaly.
entities: hemorrhagic disorder due to a platelet anomaly

he_048648.description: ventricular fibrillation, paroxysmal familial, presenting with Ventricular extrasystoles, Sudden cardiac death, Autosomal dominant inheritance, and Tachycardia. Often co-occurs with genetic cardiac rhythm disease and paroxysmal familial ventricular fibrillation.

he_048732.anchor: metabolic encephalomyopathic crises, recurrent, with rhabdomyolysis, cardiac arrhythmias, and neurodegeneration
he_048732.description: metabolic encephalomyopathic crises, recurrent, with rhabdomyolysis, cardiac arrhythmias, and neurodegeneration, presenting with Hyperactive deep tendon reflexes, Elevated serum creatine kinase, Clonus, and Cardiac arrest. Often co-occurs with Mendelian disease.
entities: metabolic encephalomyopathic crises, recurrent, with rhabdomyolysis, cardiac arrhythmias, and neurodegeneration

he_048839.description: diabetic ketoacidosis, presenting with Autoimmunity, Ketoacidosis, Insulin resistance, and Multifactorial inheritance. Treatment includes Insulin degludec, Insulin glulisine, and Insulin detemir. Contraindicated: Fosinopril and Pyrazinamide. Often co-occurs with type 2 diabetes mellitus and inborn errors of metabolism.

he_048844.description: acquired polycythemia vera, presenting with Cerebral hemorrhage, Increased megakaryocyte count, Thrombocytosis, and Thromboembolism. Treatment includes Ruxolitinib, Sodium phosphate P 32, and Uracil mustard. Contraindicated: Warfarin and Dicoumarol. Often co-occurs with erythroid neoplasm and polycythemia (disease).

he_048940.anchor: hyperammonemic encephalopathy due to carbonic anhydrase VA deficiency
he_048940.description: hyperammonemic encephalopathy due to carbonic anhydrase VA deficiency, presenting with Lethargy, Ketoacidosis, Increased serum lactate, and Global developmental delay. Often co-occurs with gluconeogenesis disorder and urea cycle disorder.
entities: hyperammonemic encephalopathy due to carbonic anhydrase VA deficiency

he_049029.anchor: methemoglobinemia due to deficiency of methemoglobin reductase
he_049029.description: methemoglobinemia due to deficiency of methemoglobin reductase, presenting with Hypertonia, Opisthotonus, Strabismus, and Global developmental delay. Contraindicated: Benzocaine and Primaquine. Often co-occurs with hereditary methemoglobinemia.
entities: methemoglobinemia due to deficiency of methemoglobin reductase

he_049220.anchor: congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 17-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency
he_049220.description: congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 17-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency, presenting with Ambiguous genitalia, Hypokalemic alkalosis, Adrenal hyperplasia, and Autosomal recessive inheritance. Often co-occurs with 46,XY disorder of sex development due to isolated 17,20-lyase deficiency and 46,XY disorder of sex development due to adrenal and testicular steroidogenesis defect.
entities: congenital adrenal hyperplasia due to 17-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency
46,XY disorder of sex development due to isolated 17,20-lyase deficiency
46,XY disorder of sex development due to adrenal and testicular steroidogenesis defect

he_049249.description: sudden cardiac arrest, presenting with Arrhythmia, Syncope, Autosomal dominant inheritance, and Sudden death. Contraindicated: Disulfiram and Phenazopyridine. Often co-occurs with sudden arrhythmia death syndrome and cardiac rhythm disease.

he_049379.description: phenformin 4-hydroxylation, presenting with Autosomal recessive inheritance and Lactic acidosis. Often co-occurs with Mendelian disease.

he_049429.description: sick sinus syndrome 2, autosomal dominant, presenting with Congenital onset, Cardiac arrest, Sick sinus syndrome, and Left ventricular noncompaction. Often co-occurs with familial sick sinus syndrome.

he_049738.description: 3-methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase 2 deficiency, presenting with Lethargy, Ketoacidosis, Alopecia, and Organic aciduria. Often co-occurs with 3-methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase deficiency.

he_049878.description: mitochondrial complex 5 (ATP synthase) deficiency nuclear, presenting with Encephalopathy, Lethargy, Elevated serum creatine kinase, and Ketoacidosis. Often co-occurs with mitochondrial complex deficiency and mitochondrial proton-transporting ATP synthase complex deficiency.
entities: mitochondrial proton-transporting ATP synthase complex deficiency

he_050015.description: inborn glycerol kinase deficiency, presenting with Lethargy, Ketoacidosis, Strabismus, and Short stature. Often co-occurs with chromosome Xp21 deletion syndrome and glycerol kinase deficiency, infantile form.

he_050039.description: corticosterone methyloxidase type 2 deficiency, presenting with Hyperkalemia, Increased circulating renin level, Failure to thrive, and Increased circulating corticosterone level. Often co-occurs with familial hyperreninemic hypoaldosteronism.

he_050049.description: cardiac arrhythmia, ankyrin-B-related, presenting with Prolonged QT interval, Syncope, Sudden cardiac death, and Autosomal dominant inheritance. Often co-occurs with familial long QT syndrome.

he_050099.description: pseudohyperaldosteronism type 2, presenting with Decreased circulating renin level, Decreased circulating aldosterone level, and Autosomal dominant inheritance. Often co-occurs with hypertension, pregnancy-induced and hypertension.

he_050116.description: 3-hydroxyisobutyric aciduria, presenting with Episodic ketoacidosis, Ketoacidosis, Aminoaciduria, and Failure to thrive. Often co-occurs with valine metabolism disease and inherited amino acid metabolic disorder.

he_050156.anchor: autosomal dominant progressive nephropathy with hypertension
he_050156.description: autosomal dominant progressive nephropathy with hypertension, presenting with Stage 5 chronic kidney disease, Progressive, Nephritis, and Renal insufficiency. Often co-occurs with hypertension.
entities: autosomal dominant progressive nephropathy with hypertension

he_050461.anchor: ketoacidosis due to monocarboxylate transporter-1 deficiency
he_050461.description: ketoacidosis due to monocarboxylate transporter-1 deficiency, presenting with Ketoacidosis, Ketonuria, Feeding difficulties, and Global developmental delay. Often co-occurs with disorder of ketone body transport.
entities: ketoacidosis due to monocarboxylate transporter-1 deficiency

he_050526.description: Addison disease, presenting with Abnormality of skin pigmentation, Vomiting, Seizure, and Hyperkalemia. Treatment includes Methylprednisolone, Prednisone, and Prednisolone. Contraindicated: Benzoyl peroxide and Guaifenesin. Often co-occurs with adrenocortical hypofunction, chronic primary congenital and adrenocortical insufficiency.

he_050561.anchor: pyogenic bacterial infections due to MyD88 deficiency
he_050561.description: pyogenic bacterial infections due to MyD88 deficiency, presenting with T lymphocytopenia, Recurrent meningitis, Recurrent skin infections, and Abnormal natural killer cell count. Often co-occurs with hereditary predisposition to infections.
entities: pyogenic bacterial infections due to MyD88 deficiency

he_050590.description: IDDM 1, presenting with Autoimmunity, Ketoacidosis, Polydipsia, and Polyphagia. Treatment includes Insulin glargine, Insulin lispro, and Insulin human. Contraindicated: Dichlorophen and Diethylpropion. Often co-occurs with type 1 diabetes mellitus.

he_050600.description: corticosterone methyloxidase type 1 deficiency, presenting with Vomiting, Hyperkalemia, Increased circulating renin level, and Failure to thrive. Often co-occurs with familial hyperreninemic hypoaldosteronism and steroid metabolism disease.

he_050623.anchor: thrombophilia due to protein S deficiency, autosomal dominant
he_050623.description: thrombophilia due to protein S deficiency, autosomal dominant, presenting with Arterial thrombosis, Hypercoagulability, Superficial thrombophlebitis, and Warfarin-induced skin necrosis. Often co-occurs with autosomal dominant disease and hereditary thrombophilia due to congenital protein S deficiency.
entities: thrombophilia due to protein S deficiency, autosomal dominant
hereditary thrombophilia due to congenital protein S deficiency

he_050881.description: pyruvate dehydrogenase E3 deficiency, presenting with Lethargy, Encephalopathy, Decreased liver function, and Feeding difficulties. Contraindicated: Benzocaine and Primaquine. Often co-occurs with syndromic dyslipidemia and maple syrup urine disease.

he_050895.description: Braddock syndrome, presenting with Epicanthus, Preaxial hand polydactyly, Abnormal hair morphology, and Unilateral renal agenesis. Often co-occurs with genetic multiple congenital anomalies/dysmorphic syndrome without intellectual disability and hypertension.
entities: genetic multiple congenital anomalies/dysmorphic syndrome without intellectual disability

he_081915.description: Complications of neonatal diabetes mellitus: Long-term complications of diabetes develop gradually. The longer you have diabetes — and the less controlled your blood sugar — the higher the risk of complications. Eventually, diabetes complications may be disabling or even life-threatening. Possible complications include: Cardiovascular disease. Diabetes dramatically increases the risk of various cardiovascular problems, including coronary artery disease with chest pain, heart attack, stroke and narrowing of arteries . If you have diabetes, you're more likely to have heart disease or stroke., Nerve damage . Excess sugar can injure the walls of the tiny blood vessels that nourish your nerves, especially in your legs. This can cause tingling, numbness, burning or pain that usually begins at the tips of the toes or fingers and gradually spreads upward. Left untreated, you could lose all sense of feeling in the affected limbs. Damage to the nerves related to digestion can cause problems with nausea, vomiting, diarrhea or constipation. For men, it may lead to erectile dysfunction., Kidney damage . The kidneys contain millions of tiny blood vessel clusters that filter waste from your blood.

he_082014.description: Management of malignant hyperthermia of anesthesia: Dantrolene sodium is a specific antagonist of the pathophysiologic changes in MH and should be available wherever general anesthesia is administered. When MH is suspected, administration of triggering agents should be ceased immediately and anesthesia continued with intravenous propofol, opiods and/or sedatives. During an MH crisis, dantrolene should be administered at a dosage of 2.5 mg/kg, every 5-10 minutes until patient is stabilized. Volume resuscitation and administration of vasopressors might be needed to stabilize hemodynamics. Cooling (with cold intravenous fluids, topical ice or special cooling blankets) is essential as high temperatures exacerbate an established MH reaction.

Diabetes dramatically increases the risk of various cardiovascular problems, including coronary artery disease with chest pain, heart attack, stroke and narrowing of arteries . If you have diabetes, you're more likely to have heart disease or stroke., Nerve damage . Excess sugar can injure the walls of the tiny blood vessels that nourish your nerves, especially in your legs. This can cause tingling, numbness, burning or pain that usually begins at the tips of the toes or fingers and gradually spreads upward. Left untreated, you could lose all sense of feeling in the affected limbs. Damage to the nerves related to digestion can cause problems with nausea, vomiting, diarrhea or constipation. For men, it may lead to erectile dysfunction., Kidney damage . The kidneys contain millions of tiny blood vessel clusters that filter waste from your blood.

Plaque buildup causes narrowed or blocked blood vessels that can lead to a heart attack, chest pain or stroke. Coronary artery disease symptoms may be different for men and women. For instance, men are more likely to have chest pain. Women are more likely to have other signs and symptoms along with chest discomfort, such as shortness of breath, nausea and extreme fatigue. Signs and symptoms can include: Chest pain, chest tightness, chest pressure and chest discomfort, Shortness of breath, Pain, numbness, weakness or coldness in your legs or arms if the blood vessels in those parts of your body are narrowed, Pain in the neck, jaw, throat, upper abdomen or back

he_085843.description: meningococcal infection: An acute bacterial disease caused by Neisseria meningitides that presents usually, but not always, with a rash (non blanching petechial or purpuric rash), progressively developing signs of meningitis (fever, vomiting, headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness) and later leading to confusion, delirium and drowsiness. Neck stiffness and photophobia are often absent in infants and young children who may manifest nonspecific signs such as irritability, inconsolable crying, poor feeding, and a bulging fontanel. Meningococcal meningitis may also present as part of early or late onset sepsis in neonates. The disease is potentially fatal. Surviving patients may develop neurological sequelae that include sensorineural hearing loss, seizures, spasticity, attention deficits and intellectual disability.

he_085844.description: meningococcal infection: A fulminant infection of the meninges and subarachnoid fluid by the bacterium neisseria meningitidis, producing diffuse inflammation and peri-meningeal venous thromboses. Clinical manifestations include fever, nuchal rigidity, seizures, severe headache, petechial rash, stupor, focal neurologic deficits, hydrocephalus, and coma. The organism is usually transmitted via nasopharyngeal secretions and is a leading cause of meningitis in children and young adults. Organisms from Neisseria meningitidis serogroups a, b, c, y, and w-135 have been reported to cause meningitis. :13-8)

he_085845.description: meningococcal infection: Meningococcal meningitis is an acute bacterial disease caused by Neisseria meningitides that presents usually, but not always, with a rash (non blanching petechial or purpuric rash), progressively developing signs of meningitis (fever, vomiting, headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness) and later leading to confusion, delirium and drowsiness. Neck stiffness and photophobia are often absent in infants and young children who may manifest nonspecific signs such as irritability, inconsolable crying, poor feeding, and a bulging fontanel. Meningococcal meningitis may also present as part of early or late onset sepsis in neonates. The disease is potentially fatal. Surviving patients may develop neurological sequelae that include sensorineural hearing loss, seizures, spasticity, attention deficits and intellectual disability.

he_085846.description: Symptoms of meningococcal infection: Early meningitis symptoms may mimic the flu . Symptoms may develop over several hours or over a few days. Possible signs and symptoms in anyone older than the age of 2 include: Sudden high fever, Stiff neck, Severe headache that seems different from normal, Headache with nausea or vomiting, Confusion or difficulty concentrating, Seizures, Sleepiness or difficulty waking, Sensitivity to light, No appetite or thirst, Skin rash, Signs in newborns, Newborns and infants may show these signs: High fever, Constant crying, Excessive sleepiness or irritability, Difficulty waking from sleep, Inactivity or sluggishness, Not waking to eat, Poor feeding, Vomiting, A bulge in the soft spot on top of a baby's head, Stiffness in the body and neck, Infants with meningitis may be difficult to comfort, and may even cry harder when held.

he_085847.description: Causes of meningococcal infection: Viral infections are the most common cause of meningitis, followed by bacterial infections and, rarely, fungal and parasitic infections. Because bacterial infections can be life-threatening, identifying the cause is essential. Bacterial meningitis, Bacteria that enter the bloodstream and travel to the brain and spinal cord cause acute bacterial meningitis. But it can also occur when bacteria directly invade the meninges. This may be caused by an ear or sinus infection, a skull fracture, or — rarely — some surgeries. Several strains of bacteria can cause acute bacterial meningitis, most commonly: Streptococcus pneumoniae . This bacterium is the most common cause of bacterial meningitis in infants, young children and adults in the United States. It more commonly causes pneumonia or ear or sinus infections. A vaccine can help prevent this infection. Neisseria meningitidis . This bacterium is another leading cause of bacterial meningitis. These bacteria commonly cause an upper respiratory infection but can cause meningococcal meningitis when they enter the bloodstream. This is a highly contagious infection that affects mainly teenagers and young adults.

he_085848.description: Risk factors for meningococcal infection: Risk factors for meningitis include: Skipping vaccinations. Risk rises for anyone who hasn't completed the recommended childhood or adult vaccination schedule. Age. Most cases of viral meningitis occur in children younger than age 5. Bacterial meningitis is common in those under age 20. Living in a community setting. College students living in dormitories, personnel on military bases, and children in boarding schools and child care facilities are at greater risk of meningococcal meningitis. This is probably because the bacterium is spread through the respiratory route, and spreads quickly through large groups. Pregnancy. Pregnancy increases the risk of listeriosis — an infection caused by listeria bacteria, which may also cause meningitis. Listeriosis increases the risk of miscarriage, stillbirth and premature delivery. Compromised immune system. AIDS, alcoholism, diabetes, use of immunosuppressant drugs and other factors that affect your immune system also make you more susceptible to meningitis. Having your spleen removed also increases your risk, and anyone without a spleen should get vaccinated to minimize that risk.

he_085849.description: Complications of meningococcal infection: Meningitis complications can be severe. The longer you or your child has the disease without treatment, the greater the risk of seizures and permanent neurological damage, including: Hearing loss, Memory difficulty, Learning disabilities, Brain damage, Gait problems, Seizures, Kidney failure, Shock, Death, With prompt treatment, even people with severe meningitis can have good recovery.

he_085850.description: Prevention of meningococcal infection: Common bacteria or viruses that can cause meningitis can spread through coughing, sneezing, kissing, or sharing eating utensils, a toothbrush or a cigarette. These steps can help prevent meningitis: Wash your hands. Careful hand-washing helps prevent the spread of germs. Teach children to wash their hands often, especially before eating and after using the toilet, spending time in a crowded public place or petting animals. Show them how to vigorously and thoroughly wash and rinse their hands. Practice good hygiene. Don't share drinks, foods, straws, eating utensils, lip balms or toothbrushes with anyone else. Teach children and teens to avoid sharing these items too. Stay healthy. Maintain your immune system by getting enough rest, exercising regularly, and eating a healthy diet with plenty of fresh fruits, vegetables and whole grains. Cover your mouth. When you need to cough or sneeze, be sure to cover your mouth and nose. If you're pregnant, take care with food. Reduce your risk of listeriosis by cooking meat, including hot dogs and deli meat, to 165 F . Avoid cheeses made from unpasteurized milk. Choose cheeses that are clearly labeled as being made with pasteurized milk.

he_085851.description: When to seek care for meningococcal infection: When to see a doctor, Seek immediate medical care if you or someone in your family has meningitis signs or symptoms, such as: Fever, Severe, unrelenting headache, Confusion, Vomiting, Stiff neck, Bacterial meningitis is serious and can be fatal within days without prompt antibiotic treatment. Delayed treatment increases the risk of permanent brain damage or death. It's also important to talk to your doctor if a family member or someone you live or work with has meningitis. You may need to take medications to prevent getting the infection.

he_085922.description: pneumococcal meningitis: An acute purulent infection of the meninges and subarachnoid space caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, most prevalent in children and adults over the age of 60. This illness may be associated with otitis media; mastoiditis; sinusitis; respiratory tract infections; sickle cell disease (anemia, sickle cell); skull fractures; and other disorders. Clinical manifestations include fever; headache; neck stiffness; and somnolence followed by seizures; focal neurologic deficits (notably deafness); and coma. (From Miller et al., Merritt's Textbook of Neurology, 9th ed, p111)

he_085923.description: pneumococcal meningitis: Meningitis that is attributed to the bacteria Streptococcus pneumonia.

he_085924.description: pneumococcal meningitis: A rare infectious disease of the nervous system caused by the bacterium Streptococcus pneumoniae, which is commonly part of the bacterial flora colonizing the nasopharyngeal mucosa. The disease is clinically characterized by typical symptoms of acute leptomeningitis, like fever, headache, neck stiffness, vomiting, and clouding of consciousness. It is frequently fatal and, in surviving patients, often accompanied by long-term sequelae, especially focal neurological deficits, hearing loss, cognitive impairment, and epilepsy.

he_087053.description: allergic asthma: A asthma with a basis in a pathological type I hypersensitivity reaction.

he_087054.description: allergic asthma: Asthma is characterized by increased responsiveness of the tracheobronchial tree to multiple stimuli, leading to narrowing of the air passages with resultant dyspnea, cough, and wheezing.

he_087055.description: Symptoms of allergic asthma: Asthma symptoms vary from person to person. You may have infrequent asthma attacks, have symptoms only at certain times — such as when exercising — or have symptoms all the time. Asthma signs and symptoms include: Shortness of breath, Chest tightness or pain, Wheezing when exhaling, which is a common sign of asthma in children, Trouble sleeping caused by shortness of breath, coughing or wheezing, Coughing or wheezing attacks that are worsened by a respiratory virus, such as a cold or the flu, Signs that your asthma is probably worsening include: Asthma signs and symptoms that are more frequent and bothersome, Increasing difficulty breathing, as measured with a device used to check how well your lungs are working, The need to use a quick-relief inhaler more often, For some people, asthma signs and symptoms flare up in certain situations: Exercise-induced asthma, which may be worse when the air is cold and dry, Occupational asthma, triggered by workplace irritants such as chemical fumes, gases or dust, Allergy-induced asthma, triggered by airborne substances, such as pollen, mold spores, cockroach waste, or particles of skin and dried saliva shed by pets

he_087056.description: Causes of allergic asthma: It isn't clear why some people get asthma and others don't, but it's probably due to a combination of environmental and inherited factors. Asthma triggers, Exposure to various irritants and substances that trigger allergies can trigger signs and symptoms of asthma. Asthma triggers are different from person to person and can include: Airborne allergens, such as pollen, dust mites, mold spores, pet dander or particles of cockroach waste, Respiratory infections, such as the common cold, Physical activity, Cold air, Air pollutants and irritants, such as smoke, Certain medications, including beta blockers, aspirin, and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, such as ibuprofen and naproxen sodium, Strong emotions and stress, Sulfites and preservatives added to some types of foods and beverages, including shrimp, dried fruit, processed potatoes, beer and wine, Gastroesophageal reflux disease, a condition in which stomach acids back up into your throat

he_087057.description: Risk factors for allergic asthma: A number of factors are thought to increase your chances of developing asthma. They include: Having a blood relative with asthma, such as a parent or sibling, Having another allergic condition, such as atopic dermatitis — which causes red, itchy skin — or hay fever — which causes a runny nose, congestion and itchy eyes, Being overweight, Being a smoker, Exposure to secondhand smoke, Exposure to exhaust fumes or other types of pollution, Exposure to occupational triggers, such as chemicals used in farming, hairdressing and manufacturing

he_087058.description: Complications of allergic asthma: Asthma complications include: Signs and symptoms that interfere with sleep, work and other activities, Sick days from work or school during asthma flare-ups, A permanent narrowing of the tubes that carry air to and from your lungs, which affects how well you can breathe, Emergency room visits and hospitalizations for severe asthma attacks, Side effects from long-term use of some medications used to stabilize severe asthma, Proper treatment makes a big difference in preventing both short-term and long-term complications caused by asthma.

he_087059.description: Prevention of allergic asthma: While there's no way to prevent asthma, you and your doctor can design a step-by-step plan for living with your condition and preventing asthma attacks. Follow your asthma action plan. With your doctor and health care team, write a detailed plan for taking medications and managing an asthma attack. Then be sure to follow your plan. Asthma is an ongoing condition that needs regular monitoring and treatment. Taking control of your treatment can make you feel more in control of your life., Get vaccinated for influenza and pneumonia. Staying current with vaccinations can prevent flu and pneumonia from triggering asthma flare-ups. Identify and avoid asthma triggers. A number of outdoor allergens and irritants — ranging from pollen and mold to cold air and air pollution — can trigger asthma attacks. Find out what causes or worsens your asthma, and take steps to avoid those triggers., Monitor your breathing. You may learn to recognize warning signs of an impending attack, such as slight coughing, wheezing or shortness of breath. But because your lung function may decrease before you notice any signs or symptoms, regularly measure and record your peak airflow with a home peak flow meter.

he_087060.description: When to seek care for allergic asthma: When to see a doctor, Seek emergency treatment, Severe asthma attacks can be life-threatening. Work with your doctor to determine what to do when your signs and symptoms worsen — and when you need emergency treatment. Signs of an asthma emergency include: Rapid worsening of shortness of breath or wheezing, No improvement even after using a quick-relief inhaler, Shortness of breath when you are doing minimal physical activity, Contact your doctor, See your doctor: If you think you have asthma. If you have frequent coughing or wheezing that lasts more than a few days or any other signs or symptoms of asthma, see your doctor. Treating asthma early may prevent long-term lung damage and help keep the condition from getting worse over time. To monitor your asthma after diagnosis. If you know you have asthma, work with your doctor to keep it under control. Good long-term control helps you feel better from day to day and can prevent a life-threatening asthma attack., If your asthma symptoms get worse. Contact your doctor right away if your medication doesn't seem to ease your symptoms or if you need to use your quick-relief inhaler more often.
